title: Effect of Co-Inoculation With Rhizosphere and Endophytic Bacteria on Growth and Soil Microbial Structure of Sesbania cannabina Under Salt Stress.
author_string: Pu, Zitian, Wang, Yanxia, Zhang, Congzhi, Zhou, Yanan, Wang, Dandan et al.
abstract: Combined application of plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria with endophytic bacteria to improve plant salt tolerance has remained unclear. In this study, salt-tolerant bacteria isolated from the rhizosphere saline soil of Sesbania cannabina were assessed for their effects on seed germination and plant growth under salt stress. Low, medium, and high salt stress levels (0, 300, and 400 mM NaCl) were established, and the effects of four microbial treatments (no addition; rhizobacteria Klebsiella aerogenes K64; endophytic bacteria Bacillus velezensis SC35; and combined K. aerogenes and B. velezensis) on salt tolerance and soil microbial abundance in S. cannabina were evaluated. The results showed that K. aerogenes exhibited multiple growth-promoting traits and could tolerate high NaCl concentrations. Co-inoculation with K. aerogenes and B. velezensis significantly enhanced shoot and root biomass, chlorophyll content, and nodule number under all salt stress levels. Co-inoculation with K. aerogenes and B. velezensis also increased antioxidant enzyme activity and proline content while reducing malondialdehyde accumulation. Under high salt stress, co-inoculation regulated the abundance of Proteobacteria, Actinobacteriota, and Bacteroidota, enhancing the functional potential of soil microbial communities. In the absence of exogenous bacteria, the correlations among S. cannabina traits were stronger. However, the associations between key microorganisms and plant traits were relatively simple. With bacterial inoculation, plant trait correlations were weakened, while correlations between key microorganisms and S. cannabina traits were strengthened. In conclusion, co-inoculation with K. aerogenes and B. velezensis significantly improved the salt tolerance of S. cannabina, suggesting that its salt-tolerance strategy largely relies on microbial assistance.
